text: This is a case of a forty-three year old female who presented to me with a complex midline incisional hernia. She had previously had an exploratory laparotomy for a perforated duodenal ulcer for which she underwent a grand patch and then subsequently developed acute cholecystitis and had a cholecystectomy. She presented to me with this CT scan in a large midline incisional hernia and so I plan to do a transversus abdominis release.

This is a case of a forty-three year old female who presented to me with a complex midline incisional hernia. She had previously had an exploratory laparotomy for a perforated duodenal ulcer for which she underwent a grand patch and then subsequently developed acute cholecystitis and had a cholecystectomy. She presented to me with this CT scan in a large midline incisional hernia and so I plan to do a transversus abdominis release. As you can see here, she has a large, complex incisional hernia. Not a significant amount of adhesions, but she did have adhesions mainly in the upper portion of the hernia defect. The majority of these were omental adhesions. But as you can see, adhesiolysis was pretty straightforward as there really was no intestinal contribution to the adhesions.
The video shows a large, complex incisional hernia with minimal adhesions, primarily located in the upper portion of the hernia defect. Most of the adhesions are omental.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, revealing the liver densely adherent to the anterior abdominal wall near the subxiphoid region. The dissection aims to develop the retromuscular space and incise the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring adequate cephaloid overlap for mesh placement.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully dissecting the liver from the anterior abdominal wall in the subxiphoid region. The omentum is lowered, and part of the liver is separated from the abdominal wall to develop the retromuscular space and incise the posterior rectus sheath. During dissection, the surgeon encounters an unexpected structure, which is not the colon or stomach, and checks the pylorus and duodenum. To prevent contamination, a Raytech is used to collect any contents without spillage.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully dissecting the omentum and part of the liver from the anterior abdominal wall. During dissection, an unexpected structure is encountered, which is not the colon or stomach, as these organs are not in proximity. The surgeon examines the area near the pylorus and the first portion of the duodenum, confirming that the structure is not bowel-related.

The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully collecting a paste-like material into a Raytech sponge to avoid contamination. The material is contained within an EndoCatch bag and the Raytech, preventing spillage. The surgeon cleans the area, suspecting the material to be a retained gallstone from a previous cholecystectomy.

The surgeon expresses a material resembling anchovy paste, believed to be a retained gallstone, from a crater near the liver bed. The material is contained within an EndoCatch bag and Raytech, with no spillage. The liver bed is cauterized to remove any residue, and clean instruments are used for further dissection. A small portion of the liver is dissected from the anterior abdominal wall to facilitate incision of the posterior rectus sheath in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon removes the Raytech containing the contents and cauterizes the liver bed to eliminate any residue. The EndoCatch bag is extracted, and clean instruments are used. A small portion of the liver is dissected from the anterior abdominal wall, aiming to incise the posterior rectus sheath in the upper abdomen for preperitoneal and subxiphoid dissection.

The surgeon separates the liver from the anterior abdominal wall with dissection, then begins incising the posterior rectus sheath just off midline. This incision marks the start of the retromuscular dissection, with the incision approaching the liver edge.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath in the upper abdomen, staying just off midline, to initiate retromuscular dissection. The incision is made close to the liver edge, but the surgeon successfully avoids injury to neurovascular bundles while developing the retromuscular space laterally towards the linea semilunaris.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath along the planned dissection length in the upper abdomen, carefully developing the retromuscular plane laterally towards the linea semilunaris while avoiding injury to neurovascular bundles. Despite limitations posed by the liver, the incision continues over the liver area.

The surgeon carefully preserves a large neurovascular bundle while performing a retromuscular dissection in the upper abdomen. The dissection extends into the suprapubic area, taking down the posterior rectus sheath below the arcuate line and entering the preperitoneal plane towards the space of Retzius.

The surgeon performs a retromuscular dissection in the upper abdomen, incising the posterior rectus sheath and extending the dissection below the arcuate line into the preperitoneal plane. The dissection progresses towards the space of Retzius, revealing the pubic bone and round ligament. The transversalis fascia is incised, and a lateral tunnel is created, exposing the transversus abdominis muscle fibers and separating them from the posterior elements.

The surgeon incises the transversalis fascia to access the pre-peritoneal plane, revealing the pubic bone below. The round ligament is visible but remains undivided as the dissection progresses laterally.

The surgeon creates a lateral tunnel, identifying the transversus abdominis muscle fibers and separating them from the preserved posterior elements, initiating the bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The surgeon begins a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR), separating the transversus abdominis muscle fibers from posterior components, moving from right to left. The posterior lamella of the internal oblique is opened, and the transversalis fascia is left intact in the upper abdomen for added strength. Dissection continues laterally, staying medial to neurovascular bundles to prevent lateral hernia defects. The bulky transversus abdominis transitions from muscular to aponeurotic, meeting the inferior dissection plane and separating from the transversalis fascia. The transition between the preperitoneal and pre-transversalis spaces is visible.

The surgeon separates the transversus abdominis muscle fibers from the posterior components, moving from right to left in the lower abdomen. The transversalis fascia remains intact as the dissection progresses to the upper abdomen, where the posterior lamella of the internal oblique is opened, revealing the thin peritoneum layer.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the transversus abdominis muscle, separating it from the posterior elements while staying medial to the neurovascular bundles. The dissection progresses from top down, aiming to connect the upper and lower TAR dissections. The transversus abdominis appears bulky, transitioning from muscular to aponeurotic as the dissection approaches the inferior area.

The surgeon separates the transversus abdominis muscle from the transversalis fascia, revealing the transition between the preperitoneal and pre-transversalis spaces. The dissection progresses from the upper abdomen, where the transversalis fascia is below, to the lower abdomen, where it is on the transversus abdominis muscle. The lateral dissection extends posteriorly towards the psoas muscle.

The surgeon continues dissection on the transversus abdominis muscle towards the lower abdomen, extending laterally to the psoas muscle if necessary. The posterior rectus sheath is positioned atop the viscera without tension. The diaphragm fibers are visible as they meet the transversus abdominis fibers. The liver, previously adherent to the posterior sheath, is successfully separated. The surgeon opens in the cephalad direction to ensure adequate mesh overlap. A small hole in the peritoneum is closed with a figure-of-eight suture. The surgeon places contralateral ports and completes the right-sided TAR, preparing for the left-sided dissection. The incision of the posterior sheath begins on the left side, extending into the subxiphoid space in the preperitoneal plane above the liver, ensuring at least five centimeters of mesh overlap above the defect.

The surgeon continues dissection in the upper abdomen, revealing diaphragm fibers meeting transversus abdominis fibers. The liver, initially adherent to the posterior rectus sheath, is gently separated and moved down. The dissection progresses cephalad to ensure adequate mesh overlap, with partial exploration into the space of Retzius. A small hole in the peritoneum is closed using a figure-of-eight suture technique. Contralateral ports are placed as the right-sided TAR is completed.

The surgeon flips the boom and redocks to perform the left-sided dissection, staying in the preperitoneal space. The incision is made in the peritoneum to maintain tissue integrity for potential visceral coverage. The posterior sheath is incised, and dissection proceeds into the subxiphoid space above the liver, extending at least five centimeters for mesh overlap. The dissection continues along the posterior rectus sheath into the lower abdomen.

The surgeon continues dissection along the posterior rectus sheath into the lower abdomen, entering the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line. The dissection meets the right-sided and suprapubic crossover dissection. Transversus abdominis muscle fibers are visible laterally, and the surgeon preserves neurovascular bundles while extending the retromuscular dissection to the semilunar line. The round ligament is seen on the patient's left side, and the diaphragm fibers are kept above to prevent a Morgagni hernia. The posterior sheath is separated from the transversus abdominis muscle, transitioning from the pre-transversalis to the pre-peritoneal space.

The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line, connecting with the right-sided dissection and suprapubic crossover. The retromuscular dissection is extended laterally towards the semilunar line, with visible transversus abdominis fibers in the upper abdomen. Neurovascular bundles are preserved during the dissection.

The surgeon dissects around the port sites in the lower abdomen, closing the three port holes with a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure of eight fashion. In the preperitoneal space, the round ligament is visible on the patient's left side. The surgeon separates the posterior elements from the lateral abdominal wall, exposing the transversus abdominis muscle fibers laterally. A tunnel is created, and the surgeon opts for a top-down transversus abdominis release (TAR), ensuring the diaphragm fibers remain above to prevent a iatrogenic Morgagni hernia.

The surgeon opens the posterior lamella of the internal oblique on the left side, dividing the transversus abdominis muscle fibers to enter the plane and separate components from the posterior elements in the lateral abdominal wall. The diaphragm is visible, with muscle fibers kept elevated. The posterior sheath is removed from the raw transversus abdominis muscle, transitioning from the pre-transversalis to the pre-peritoneal space as the dissection moves inferiorly. The aponeurotic portion of the transversus abdominis is opened near a port site, completing the dissection and connecting the upper and lower dissections.

The surgeon re-approximates the posterior rectus sheath using a two-oh V-lock suture, ensuring the layer comes together without tension. The falciform ligament is utilized to support the closure due to the weight of the liver. The surgeon begins suturing from below, meeting the sutures in the middle, and ties them together. The port site holes in the peritoneum are closed using a three ovicle suture in a figure of eight fashion.

The surgeon uses a two-oh V-lock suture to close the posterior layer without tension, utilizing parts of the falciform ligament to re-approximate both sides due to the heavy liver. The port site holes in the peritoneum are closed with a three ovicle suture in a figure of eight fashion. For larger defects, a number one non-absorbable V-lock suture is used, taking multiple bites of the linea alba and hernia sac to obliterate dead space. The pneumoperitoneum is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury during the re-approximation of the anterior rectus sheath, ensuring careful attention to avoid tenting or dimpling of the skin.

The surgeon closes the defect using a number one non-absorbable V-lock suture, incorporating parts of the linea alba and hernia sac to obliterate dead space. The suture is tightened sequentially, with the needle anchored in the fascia to facilitate the process.

The surgeon takes multiple bites to approximate the anterior rectus sheath edges on each side of the rectus muscle, using number one V-lock sutures. The pneumoperitoneum is decreased to eight millimeters of mercury during this re-approximation process.

The surgeon works to transform a large football-shaped defect into a smaller ellipse-shaped defect by taking careful bites of the hernia sac. The skin above is monitored closely to avoid buttonholing or dimpling, ensuring shallow bites to obliterate the space and minimize seroma formation. A drain is left in place to assist with fluid management.

The surgeon carefully takes bites of the fascia without involving much of the muscle, using a V-lock suture to sequentially tighten along the incision. The needle is kept in place to facilitate quick retrieval, and the suture is sewn back on itself to reinforce potentially weak areas, especially in patients with thin fascia.

The surgeon places a 30 by 30 centimeter bioabsorbable mesh within the retromuscular space, ensuring it lays flat. A fibrin sealant is sprayed over the mesh, and a drain is placed to complete the procedure.

The surgeon measures the retromuscular space and places a 30x30 cm bioabsorbable mesh, Phasix, within it. The mesh is laid smoothly, followed by the application of fibrin sealant and placement of a drain to complete the procedure.
